children.value: , extending them to nonlinear dynamics parameterized by neural networks.
Let’s turn our attention back to models for sequential data for the next few lectures. With all the excitement around large language modeling, this is a timely topic.

children.html: x1:T=(x1,…,xT)\mbx_{1:T} = (\mbx_1, \ldots, \mbx_T)x1:T​=(x1​,…,xT​)
xt∈RD\mbx_t \in \reals^Dxt​∈RD

children.value:
.
We can always factor a joint distribution over observation into a product of conditionals using the chain rule,

children.html: p(x1:T)=p(x1)∏t=2Tp(xt∣x1:t−1).\begin{align*} p(\mbx_{1:T}) &= p(\mbx_1) \prod_{t=2}^T p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}). \end{align*}p(x1:T​)​=p(x1​)t=2∏T​p(xt​∣x1:t−1​).​
xt\mbx_txt​
x1,…,xt−1\mbx_{1}, \ldots, \mbx_{t-1}x1​,…,xt−1​

children.value: Autoregressive models are well-suited to sequential modeling since they make forward generation or

easy.
As long as we have access to the conditionals, we can sample forward indefinitely.

The question is, how should we parameterize these conditional distributions?
It looks challenging since each one takes in a variable-length history of previous observations.
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are autoregressive models in which the conditional distributions are functions of a finite-dimensional

children.html: ht∈RK\mbh_t \in \reals^Kht​∈RK
p(xt∣x1:t−1)=p(xt∣g(ht;θ)).\begin{align*} p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}) &= p(\mbx_t \mid g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta)). \end{align*}p(xt​∣x1:t−1​)​=p(xt​∣g(ht​;θ)).​

children.value: The hidden state is updated with each new observation as,

children.html: ht+1=f(ht,xt;θ).\begin{align*} \mbh_{t+1} &= f(\mbh_t, \mbx_t; \mbtheta). \end{align*}ht+1​​=f(ht​,xt​;θ).​

children.value: The conditional distribution over the next observation is a function of hidden state. The hidden state is updated recursively, and its size is fixed regardless of the sequence length.
The standard, “vanilla” RNN consists of a linear-nonlinear state update. For example,

children.html: f(ht,xt;θ)=tanh⁡(Wht+Bxt),\begin{align*} f(\mbh_t, \mbx_t; \mbtheta) &= \tanh \left(\mbW \mbh_t + \mbB \mbx_t \right), \end{align*}f(ht​,xt​;θ)​=tanh(Wht​+Bxt​),​
W∈RK×K\mbW \in \reals^{K \times K}W∈RK×K
B∈RK×D\mbB \in \reals^{K \times D}B∈RK×D
tanh⁡(⋅)\tanh(\cdot)tanh(⋅)

children.value: Hyperbolic Tangent and the Logistic Function
The hyperbolic tangent function equivalent is typically written as,

children.html: tanh⁡(a)=ea−e−aea+e−a.\begin{align*} \tanh(a) &= \frac{e^a - e^{-a}}{e^a + e^{-a}}. \end{align*}tanh(a)​=ea+e−aea−e−a​.​

tanh⁡(a)=1−2e−aea+e−a=1−2e2a+1=1−2σ(−2a)=1−2(1−σ(2a))=2σ(2a)−1.\begin{align*} \tanh(a) &= 1 - \frac{2e^{-a}}{e^a + e^{-a}} \\ &= 1 - \frac{2}{e^{2a} + 1} \\ &= 1 - 2 \sigma(-2a) \\ &= 1 - 2 (1 - \sigma(2a)) \\ &= 2 \sigma(2a) - 1. \end{align*}tanh(a)​=1−ea+e−a2e−a​=1−e2a+12​=1−2σ(−2a)=1−2(1−σ(2a))=2σ(2a)−1.​

children.value: Thus, we see that the the hyperbolic tangent is simply a scaled and shifted logistic function.
The “read-out” of a vanilla RNN is typically a simple linear or generalized linear model, depending on the type of observations. For example,

children.html: g(ht,xt;θ)=Cht+d,\begin{align*} g(\mbh_t, \mbx_t; \mbtheta) &= \mbC \mbh_t + \mbd, \end{align*}g(ht​,xt​;θ)​=Cht​+d,​
C∈RD×K\mbC \in \reals^{D \times K}C∈RD×K
d∈RD\mbd \in \reals^Dd∈RD
θ=(W,B,C,d)\mbtheta = (\mbW, \mbB, \mbC, \mbd)θ=(W,B,C,d)

children.value: From a machine learning perspective, RNNs are useful function approximators for sequential data. From a neuroscience perspective, they have a long history as theoretical models of neural computation.
In such models, the hidden state

children.html: ht∈RK\mbh_t \in \reals^Kht​∈RK
KKK

children.value: neurons. With a hyperbolic tangent nonlinearity,

children.html: ht∈(−1,1)K\mbh_t \in (-1, 1)^Kht​∈(−1,1)K

children.value: , and negative rates don’t make sense. Instead, we think of

children.html: ht\mbh_tht​

children.value: as an additive offset to a baseline firing rate.

, with positive weights as excitatory synapses and negative weights as inhibitory. When a presynaptic neuron spikes, it induces an electrical current in postsynaptic neurons. Under this interpretation, the activation

children.html: Wht\mbW \mbh_tWht​

children.value: generated by inputs from other neurons.
As a neuron receives input current, its voltage steadily increases until it reaches a
, at which point the voltage spikes and the neuron fires an
. These spikes induce currents on downstream neurons, as described above. After a cell fires, there is a short
before the neuron can spike again. Thus, there is an upper bound on firing rates, which the hyperbolic tangent is meant to capture.
Artificial RNNs are trained using stochastic gradient descent (SGD) to minimize the negative log likelihood,

\begin{align*}
\cL(\mbtheta)
&= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}) \\
&= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta)) \\
&= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(f(\mbh_{t-1}, \mbx_{t-1}; \mbtheta); \mbtheta)) \\
&= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(f(\cdots f(\mbh_{1}, \mbx_{1}; \mbtheta) \cdots, \mbx_{t-1}; \mbtheta); \mbtheta)).
\end{align*}
children.html: L(θ)=−∑t=1Tlog⁡p(xt∣x1:t−1)=−∑t=1Tlog⁡p(xt∣g(ht;θ))=−∑t=1Tlog⁡p(xt∣g(f(ht−1,xt−1;θ);θ))=−∑t=1Tlog⁡p(xt∣g(f(⋯f(h1,x1;θ)⋯ ,xt−1;θ);θ)).\begin{align*} \cL(\mbtheta) &= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}) \\ &= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta)) \\ &= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(f(\mbh_{t-1}, \mbx_{t-1}; \mbtheta); \mbtheta)) \\ &= - \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(f(\cdots f(\mbh_{1}, \mbx_{1}; \mbtheta) \cdots, \mbx_{t-1}; \mbtheta); \mbtheta)). \end{align*}L(θ)​=−t=1∑T​logp(xt​∣x1:t−1​)=−t=1∑T​logp(xt​∣g(ht​;θ))=−t=1∑T​logp(xt​∣g(f(ht−1​,xt−1​;θ);θ))=−t=1∑T​logp(xt​∣g(f(⋯f(h1​,x1​;θ)⋯,xt−1​;θ);θ)).​

children.value: Now, you would simply use automatic differentiation to compute the necessary gradients to minimize this loss, but we can gain some insight by working them out manually. With some algebra, we can show that the Jacobian of the loss with respect to the dynamics weights (other parameters are similar) is,

children.html: ∂L(θ)∂W=∑t=1T∂L(θ)∂ht∂ht∂W\begin{align*} \frac{\partial \cL(\mbtheta)}{\partial \mbW} &= \sum_{t=1}^T \frac{\partial \cL(\mbtheta)}{\partial \mbh_t} \frac{ \partial \mbh_t}{\partial \mbW} \end{align*}∂W∂L(θ)​​=t=1∑T​∂ht​∂L(θ)​∂W∂ht​​​

children.value: We need the Jacobian of the loss with respect to the hidden states. These can be computed recursively by

children.html: ∂L(θ)∂ht=∂L(θ)∂ht+1∂ht+1∂ht−∂log⁡p(xt∣g(ht;θ))∂ht\begin{align*} \frac{\partial \cL(\mbtheta)}{\partial \mbh_t} &= \frac{\partial \cL(\mbtheta)}{\partial \mbh_{t+1}} \frac{\partial \mbh_{t+1}}{\partial \mbh_t} - \frac{\partial \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta))}{\partial \mbh_t} \end{align*}∂ht​∂L(θ)​​=∂ht+1​∂L(θ)​∂ht​∂ht+1​​−∂ht​∂logp(xt​∣g(ht​;θ))​​

children.value: For a vanilla RNN, the Jacobian of the next state with respect to the current state is,

children.html: ∂ht+1∂ht=diag(1−ht+12)W\begin{align*} \frac{\partial \mbh_{t+1}}{\partial \mbh_t} &= \diag \left(1 - \mbh_{t+1}^2 \right) \mbW \end{align*}∂ht​∂ht+1​​​=diag(1−ht+12​)W​
⁣d ⁣datanh⁡(a)=1−tanh⁡(a)2\frac{\dif}{\dif a}\tanh(a) = 1 - \tanh(a)^2dad​tanh(a)=1−tanh(a)2

children.value: BPTT is a linear dynamical system
The “state” of the BPTT recursions is the Jacobian, or equivalently its transpose, the gradient

children.html: st≜(∂L(θ)∂ht)⊤\mbs_t \triangleq \left(\frac{\partial \cL(\mbtheta)}{\partial \mbh_t}\right)^\topst​≜(∂ht​∂L(θ)​)⊤

children.value: . This state obeys a linear dynamical system,

children.html: st=Atst+1+bt\begin{align*} \mbs_t &= \mbA_t \mbs_{t+1} + \mbb_t \end{align*}st​​=At​st+1​+bt​​
At=(∂ht+1∂ht)⊤\mbA_t = \left(\frac{\partial \mbh_{t+1}}{\partial \mbh_t} \right)^\topAt​=(∂ht​∂ht+1​​)⊤
bt=−(∂log⁡p(xt∣g(ht;θ))∂ht)⊤\mbb_t = - \left(\frac{\partial \log p(\mbx_t \mid g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta))}{\partial \mbh_t} \right)^\topbt​=−(∂ht​∂logp(xt​∣g(ht​;θ))​)⊤

children.value: Backpropagation is the most effective way we know of to train artificial RNNs, so it’s reasonable to think that the brain might be using a similar learning algorithm. Unfortunately, it’s not clear how the backpropagation through time algorithm could be implemented by a neural circuit. The multiplication by

children.html: At\mbA_tAt​

children.value: in the gradient recursions amounts to passing information backward across synapses, and canonical synaptic models don’t have mechanisms to do this. Recent years have seen a substantial amount of research into biologically plausible mechanisms of backpropagation.

children.html: At\mbA_tAt​
≪1\ll 1≪1

children.value: . Consider the case of a linear RNN in which the

children.html: tanh⁡\tanhtanh

children.value: is replaced with identity: then the Jacobians are

children.html: At=W⊤\mbA_t = \mbW^\topAt​=W⊤

children.value: for all time steps. If the eigenvalues of

children.html: W\mbWW

children.value: are much less than one, the gradients will decay to zero exponentially quickly, absent strong inputs

children.html: bt\mbb_tbt​

children.value: Vanishing gradients are especially problematic when

children.html: xt\mbx_txt​
xs\mbx_sxs​
s≪ts \ll ts≪t

children.value: . In that case, the hidden state must propagate information about

children.html: xs\mbx_sxs​

children.value: for many time steps during the forward pass, and likewise, the gradient must pass information backward many timesteps during the backward pass. If the weights have small eigenvalues, those gradients will decay and the learning signal will fail to propagate.
One way to combat the vanishing gradient problem is by modifying the RNN architecture. Architectures like
networks achieve this via gated units.

Ft=diag(ft,1,…,ft,K)ft,k=σ(W(f)ht−1+B(f)xt−1).\begin{align*} \mbF_t &= \diag(f_{t,1}, \ldots, f_{t,K}) \\ f_{t,k} &= \sigma(\mbW^{(f)} \mbh_{t-1} + \mbB^{(f)} \mbx_{t-1}). \end{align*}Ft​ft,k​​=diag(ft,1​,…,ft,K​)=σ(W(f)ht−1​+B(f)xt−1​).​

children.html: bt=gt⊙itgt=σ(W(g)ht−1+B(g)xt−1)it=σ(W(i)ht−1+B(i)xt−1)\begin{align*} \mbb_t &= \mbg_t \odot \mbi_t \\ \mbg_t &= \sigma(\mbW^{(g)} \mbh_{t-1} + \mbB^{(g)} \mbx_{t-1}) \\ \mbi_t &= \sigma(\mbW^{(i)} \mbh_{t-1} + \mbB^{(i)} \mbx_{t-1}) \end{align*}bt​gt​it​​=gt​⊙it​=σ(W(g)ht−1​+B(g)xt−1​)=σ(W(i)ht−1​+B(i)xt−1​)​

children.value: are gated functions of the internal state passed through a nonlinearity,

children.html: ht=ot⊙tanh⁡(ct)ot=σ(W(o)ht−1+B(o)xt−1)\begin{align*} \mbh_t &= \mbo_t \odot \tanh(\mbc_t) \\ \mbo_t &= \sigma(\mbW^{(o)} \mbh_{t-1} + \mbB^{(o)} \mbx_{t-1}) \end{align*}ht​ot​​=ot​⊙tanh(ct​)=σ(W(o)ht−1​+B(o)xt−1​)​
ot∈[0,1]K\mbo_t \in [0,1]^Kot​∈[0,1]K

children.value:
.
As in a vanilla RNN, the final prediction depends on a (generalized) linear function of the hidden state,

children.html: g(ht;θ)g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta)g(ht​;θ)

children.value: We can think of an LSTM as an RNN that operates on an extended state

children.html: (ct,ht)∈R+K×[−1,1]K(\mbc_t, \mbh_t) \in \reals_+^K \times [-1, 1]^K(ct​,ht​)∈R+K​×[−1,1]K

children.value:
.
The forget gates let the eigenvalues of

children.html: Ft\mbF_tFt​

children.value: to be close to one, allowing cell states to be propagated for long periods of time on the forward pass, and gradients to be backpropagated without vanishing on the backward pass.
There are many variants of gated RNNs. Besides the LSTM, the most commonly used in the gated recurrent unit (GRU), which has a slightly simplified architecture. See
Other Variations and Uses of RNNs

children.label: Other Variations and Uses of RNNs

children.value: We motivated RNNs from an autoregressive modeling persepective, but they are useful in other sequential data settings as well. For example, suppose we want to predict the sentiment of a review

children.html: y∈Ry \in \realsy∈R

children.value: given a variable-length sequence of input words

children.html: x1:T\mbx_{1:T}x1:T​

children.value: . We can use an RNN to summarize the input sequence in terms of a hidden state for prediction,

children.html: p(y∣x1:T)=p(y∣g(ht;θ)).\begin{align*} p(y \mid \mbx_{1:T}) &= p(y \mid g(\mbh_t; \mbtheta)). \end{align*}p(y∣x1:T​)​=p(y∣g(ht​;θ)).​

children.value: Sometimes we want to map one sequence

children.html: x1:T\mbx_{1:T}x1:T​
y1:T′\mby_{1:T'}y1:T′​

children.value: . The sequences may be of different length; e.g., when we want to translate a sentence from English to French. Again, we can train an
RNN to produce a hidden state

children.html: hT\mbh_ThT​

children.value: that then becomes the initial condition for a
RNN that generates the output sequence. Formally,

children.html: p(y1:T′∣x1:T)=∏t=1T′p(yt∣y1:t−1,x1:T)=∏t=1T′p(yt∣ht′,hT)\begin{align*} p(\mby_{1:T'} \mid \mbx_{1:T}) &= \prod_{t=1}^{T'} p(\mby_{t} \mid \mby_{1:t-1}, \mbx_{1:T}) \\ &= \prod_{t=1}^{T'} p(\mby_{t} \mid \mbh'_t, \mbh_T) \end{align*}p(y1:T′​∣x1:T​)​=t=1∏T′​p(yt​∣y1:t−1​,x1:T​)=t=1∏T′​p(yt​∣ht′​,hT​)​
hT\mbh_ThT​

children.value: is the output of an RNN that processed

children.html: x1:T\mbx_{1:T}x1:T​
ht′\mbh'_tht′​

children.value: is the state of an RNN that runs over

children.html: y1:T\mby_{1:T}y1:T​

children.value: In the example above, one challenge is that the hidden state

children.html: hT\mbh_ThT​
x1:T\mbx_{1:T}x1:T​

children.value: may not adequately represent early inputs like

children.html: x1x_1x1​

children.value: . For these purposes, you can use a

children.html: x1,…,xT\mbx_1, \ldots, \mbx_Tx1​,…,xT​
xT,…,x1\mbx_T, \ldots, \mbx_1xT​,…,x1​

children.value: to produce two hidden states at each time

children.html: ttt

children.value: . These combined states can then be passed into the decoder.
As with  deep neural networks that stack layer upon layer, we can stack RNN upon RNN to construct a deeper model. In such models, the outputs of one layer,

children.html: g(ht(i);θ(i))g(\mbh_t^{(i)}; \mbtheta^{(i)})g(ht(i)​;θ(i))

children.value: become the inputs to the next layer,

children.html: xt(i+1)\mbx_t^{(i+1)}xt(i+1)​

children.value: . Then we can backpropagate gradients through the entire stack to train the model.

It turns out, we’ve already seen an RNN in this class!
We presented Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) as latent variable models with hidden states

children.html: z1:T\mbz_{1:T}z1:T​
x1:T\mbx_{1:T}x1:T​

children.value: , but we can also view them as an autoregressive model in which

children.html: p(xt∣x1:t−1)=∑k=1Kp(zt=k∣x1:t−1) p(xt∣zt=k)=∑ztα‾t,k p(xt∣zt=k)\begin{align*} p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}) &= \sum_{k=1}^K p(z_t=k \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}) \, p(\mbx_t \mid z_t=k) \\ &= \sum_{z_t} \overline{\alpha}_{t,k} \, p(\mbx_t \mid z_t=k) \end{align*}p(xt​∣x1:t−1​)​=k=1∑K​p(zt​=k∣x1:t−1​)p(xt​∣zt​=k)=zt​∑​αt,k​p(xt​∣zt​=k)​
α‾t∈ΔK−1\overline{\mbalpha}_t \in \Delta_{K-1}αt​∈ΔK−1​

children.value: from the forward-backward algorithm. They followed a simple recursion,

children.html: α‾t+1=P⊤(α‾t⊙ltα‾t⊤lt)\begin{align*} \overline{\mbalpha}_{t+1} &= \mbP^\top \left( \frac{\overline{\mbalpha}_t \odot \mbl_t}{\overline{\mbalpha}_t^\top \mbl_t} \right) \end{align*}αt+1​​=P⊤(αt⊤​lt​αt​⊙lt​​)​
lt∈RK\mbl_t \in \reals^Klt​∈RK

children.value: is the vector of likelihoods with entries

children.html: lt,k=p(xt∣zt=k)l_{t,k} = p(\mbx_t \mid z_t=k)lt,k​=p(xt​∣zt​=k)
P∈RK×K\mbP \in \reals^{K \times K}P∈RK×K

children.value: Consider an HMM with categorical emissions,

children.html: p(xt∣zt)=Cat(xt∣czt),\begin{align*} p(\mbx_t \mid z_t) &= \mathrm{Cat}(\mbx_t \mid \mbc_{z_t}), \end{align*}p(xt​∣zt​)​=Cat(xt​∣czt​​),​
xt\mbx_txt​

children.value: is a one-hot encoding of a variable that takes values in

children.html: {1,…,V}\{1,\ldots,V\}{1,…,V}
ck∈ΔV−1\mbc_k \in \Delta_{V-1}ck​∈ΔV−1​
k=1,…,Kk=1,\ldots,Kk=1,…,K

children.value:
are pmfs.
Define the matrix of likelihoods

children.html: C∈RV×K\mbC \in \reals^{V \times K}C∈RV×K
ck\mbc_kck​
C=[∣∣c1⋯cK∣∣].\begin{align*} \mbC &= \begin{bmatrix} | & & | \\ \mbc_1 & \cdots & \mbc_K \\ | & & | \end{bmatrix}. \end{align*}C​=⎣⎡​∣c1​∣​⋯​∣cK​∣​⎦⎤​.​
θ=(P,C)\mbtheta = (\mbP, \mbC)θ=(P,C)

children.value: . (Assume the initial distribution is fixed, for simplicity.)
For a categorical HMM, we can write the likelihoods as

children.html: lt=C⊤xt\mbl_t = \mbC^\top \mbx_tlt​=C⊤xt​

children.value: so that the the forward recursions simplify to,

children.html: α‾t+1=P⊤(α‾t⊙C⊤xtα‾t⊤C⊤xt)=f(α‾t,xt;θ)\begin{align*} \overline{\mbalpha}_{t+1} &= \mbP^\top \left( \frac{\overline{\mbalpha}_t \odot \mbC^\top \mbx_t}{\overline{\mbalpha}_t^\top \mbC^\top \mbx_t} \right) \\ &= f(\overline{\mbalpha}_t, \mbx_t; \mbtheta) \end{align*}αt+1​​=P⊤(αt⊤​C⊤xt​αt​⊙C⊤xt​​)=f(αt​,xt​;θ)​

children.value: Likewise, the autoregressive distributions reduce to,

children.html: p(xt∣x1:t−1)=Cat(xt∣Cα‾t)=Cat(xt∣g(α‾t;θ)).\begin{align*} p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}) &= \mathrm{Cat}(\mbx_t \mid \mbC \overline{\mbalpha}_t) \\ &= \mathrm{Cat}(\mbx_t \mid g(\overline{\mbalpha}_t; \mbtheta)). \end{align*}p(xt​∣x1:t−1​)​=Cat(xt​∣Cαt​)=Cat(xt​∣g(αt​;θ)).​

children.value: Framed this way, a categorical HMM can be seen as a simple recurrent neural network!
A Cool Trick for Computing the Gradients

children.label: A Cool Trick for Computing the Gradients

children.value: This formulation suggests that we could estimate the parameters of an HMM by directly maximizing the log likelihood,

children.html: L(θ)=log⁡p(x1:T;θ)=∑t=1Tlog⁡p(xt∣x1:t−1;θ).\begin{align*} \cL(\mbtheta) &= \log p(\mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta) = \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid \mbx_{1:t-1}; \mbtheta). \end{align*}L(θ)​=logp(x1:T​;θ)=t=1∑T​logp(xt​∣x1:t−1​;θ).​

children.value: With automatic differentiation at our disposal, this sounds like it might be a lot easier!
Let’s pursue this idea a little further. First, we’d prefer to do unconstrained optimization, so let’s parameterize the model in terms of

children.html: log⁡P\log \mbPlogP
log⁡C\log \mbClogC

children.value: . When we need the constrained versions, we will just apply the softmax to obtain simplex vectors:

children.value: . For the RNNs above, we computed them using backpropagation through time, but here we can use an even cooler trick.
First, note that the posterior distribution in an HMM can be written as an exponential family,

children.html: p(z1:T∣x1:T;θ)=exp⁡{log⁡p(z1:T,x1:T;θ)−log⁡p(x1:T;θ)}=exp⁡{log⁡p(z1;π0)+∑t=2Tlog⁡p(zt∣zt−1;P)+∑t=1Tlog⁡p(xt∣zt;C)−log⁡p(x1:T;θ)}=exp⁡{∑k=1K⟨I[z1=k],log⁡π0,k⟩+∑t=2T∑i=1K∑j=1K⟨I[zt−1=i∧zt=j],log⁡Pi,j⟩+∑t=1T∑v=1V∑k=1K⟨I[xt=v∧zt=k],log⁡Cv,k⟩−A(θ)}\begin{align*} p(\mbz_{1:T} \mid \mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta) &= \exp \left\{ \log p(\mbz_{1:T}, \mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta) - \log p(\mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta) \right\} \\ &= \exp \left\{ \log p(z_1; \mbpi_0) + \sum_{t=2}^T \log p(z_t \mid z_{t-1}; \mbP) + \sum_{t=1}^T \log p(\mbx_t \mid z_{t}; \mbC) - \log p(\mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta) \right\} \\ &= \exp \bigg\{ \sum_{k=1}^K \langle \bbI[z_1 =k], \log \pi_{0,k} \rangle \\ &\hspace{6em} + \sum_{t=2}^T \sum_{i=1}^K \sum_{j=1}^K \langle \bbI[z_{t-1}=i \wedge z_{t} = j], \log P_{i,j} \rangle \\ &\hspace{12em} + \sum_{t=1}^T \sum_{v=1}^V \sum_{k=1}^K \langle \bbI[x_t = v \wedge z_t = k], \log C_{v,k} \rangle \\ &\hspace{18em} - A(\mbtheta) \bigg\} \end{align*}p(z1:T​∣x1:T​;θ)​=exp{logp(z1:T​,x1:T​;θ)−logp(x1:T​;θ)}=exp{logp(z1​;π0​)+t=2∑T​logp(zt​∣zt−1​;P)+t=1∑T​logp(xt​∣zt​;C)−logp(x1:T​;θ)}=exp{k=1∑K​⟨I[z1​=k],logπ0,k​⟩+t=2∑T​i=1∑K​j=1∑K​⟨I[zt−1​=i∧zt​=j],logPi,j​⟩+t=1∑T​v=1∑V​k=1∑K​⟨I[xt​=v∧zt​=k],logCv,k​⟩−A(θ)}​
A(θ)=log⁡p(x1:T;θ)A(\mbtheta) = \log p(\mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta)A(θ)=logp(x1:T​;θ)

children.value: Recall that for exponential family distributions, gradients of the log normalizer yield expected sufficient statistics. Thus,

children.html: ∂A(θ)∂log⁡Cv,k=∑t=1TI[xt=v]⋅Ep(z1:T∣x1:T;θ)[I[zt=k]]\begin{align*} \frac{\partial A(\mbtheta)}{\partial \log C_{v,k}} &= \sum_{t=1}^T \bbI[x_{t} = v] \cdot \bbE_{p(\mbz_{1:T} \mid \mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta)} \left[\bbI[z_{t}=k] \right] \end{align*}∂logCv,k​∂A(θ)​​=t=1∑T​I[xt​=v]⋅Ep(z1:T​∣x1:T​;θ)​[I[zt​=k]]​

∂A(θ)∂log⁡Pi,j=∑t=2TEp(z1:T∣x1:T;θ)[I[zt−1=i∧zt=j]]\begin{align*} \frac{\partial A(\mbtheta)}{\partial \log P_{i,j}} &= \sum_{t=2}^T \bbE_{p(\mbz_{1:T} \mid \mbx_{1:T}; \mbtheta)} \left[ \bbI[z_{t-1}=i \wedge z_{t} = j] \right] \end{align*}∂logPi,j​∂A(θ)​​=t=2∑T​Ep(z1:T​∣x1:T​;θ)​[I[zt−1​=i∧zt​=j]]​

children.value: The gradients are essentially the posterior marginals and pairwise marginals we computed in the EM algorithm!

In the M-step of EM, we solve for the parameters that satisfy the constrained optimality conditions, whereas in SGD we just take a step in the direction of the gradient. EM tends to converge must faster in practice for this reason.

## Computational Cost

What is the cost of evaluating an RNN and computing its gradients? Both the forward computation of the states and the backward computation of the gradients are $\cO(T)$ time complexity. For a vanilla RNN, the cost of the matrix-vector multiplications in the forward and backward passes are likewise $\cO(K^2)$. In terms of memory, we also need to store the states for the backward pass, which takes $\cO(TK)$ memory. 

These costs are comparble to those of inference in an HMM, as we expect from the discussion above. However, $\cO(T)$ time for evaluation and gradient calculations are still quite costly in modern machine learning pipelines, and the networks that are used most in practice mitigate these costs with clever architectural changes, as we'll discuss next.

The parallel scan relied on the linearity of the RNN dynamics. Can the same be applied to nonlinear RNNs?
It turns out that yes, in many cases we can speed up the evaluation of nonlinear RNNs using a similar trick!

children.value: This idea was proposed by Lim et al (2023) and called DEER. Gonzalez et al (2024) explained the connection to the Gauss-Newton method and proposed an extension called ELK.

Recurrent neural networks are foundational models for sequential data — both as machine learning tools and as theoretical models of neural computation. The key insight is that any autoregressive model can be parameterized through a fixed-dimensional hidden state updated recursively, and the special case of linear RNNs reveals a deep connection to convolutions and parallel scan algorithms. Gated architectures like LSTMs address the vanishing-gradient problem by allowing cell states to be propagated across many time steps. While Transformers dominate modern large language models, linear and structured state space models are now challenging that dominance by combining the expressivity of recurrent computation with the parallelizability of attention.
— replaces the sequential hidden-state update with parallel self-attention, enabling far more efficient training
— shows how linear RNNs can be made both expressive and parallelizable via structured state spaces
, Ch 10, “Sequence Modeling: Recurrent and Recursive Nets.”
